narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.60, which stands out above the typical 3.21 average, you've put in strong academic effort leading up to law school. Your LSAT score of 28.50, however, falls notably below the average of 36.70, and your Year 1 school decile ranking of 4th is lower than the average 5.71, reflecting some early challenges in undergraduate performance relative to peers. The admissions model, trained on data from countless students' paths to the bar exam, predicted a 45% probability that you would fail the bar on your first try—a risk level high enough to lead to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass and succeed professionally. Among all the factors considered, like your middle family income bracket and plans to attend full-time, your undergraduate GPA emerged as the single most influential positive force, strongly pushing the prediction toward expecting you to pass the bar by showcasing your ability to excel in rigorous coursework. Next in importance was your LSAT score, which had a notable pull in the opposite direction, nudging the model toward anticipating failure because it signals potential struggles with the intense analytical demands of law school and the bar. Rounding out the top three was your Year 1 decile ranking, which offered some helpful counterbalance by suggesting you weren't at the absolute bottom of your class, though it wasn't enough to fully offset concerns. Compared to typical students who get approved, your profile shows strengths in sustained college grades but gaps in standardized testing and early-year momentum that often predict bar success. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a person or lawyer—many overcome similar hurdles through targeted preparation. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to or above average, as it carries heavy weight in these predictions. Strengthening your application with evidence of recent academic gains, like higher decile rankings in later years, could also help. Reach out to admissions advisors for feedback, and explore prep programs proven to lift bar passage rates for students in your position. With persistence, you can reshape this story and secure a spot where you'll thrive.
